questions.text: Which of the following best describes a pure 'Term Insurance' policy?
options: It pays a lump sum regardless of when the insured dies.
It refunds all premiums paid if the insured survives.
It pays a death benefit only if the insured dies during the policy term, with no savings element.
It gives a monthly pension after the term expires.
questions.explanation: Pure term insurance is a risk-only plan. It provides the highest coverage at the lowest premium but has no survival or maturity benefit. If the insured survives, the policy expires with zero payout.

questions.text: An Endowment Assurance policy is essentially a mathematical combination of which two elements?
options: A Term Assurance and a Pure Endowment
A Whole Life Policy and a Health Rider
A Money-Back Policy and a Unit Linked plan
A Pension Plan and a Term Plan
questions.explanation: An Endowment policy guarantees a payout whether the insured dies (Term Assurance part) or survives till maturity (Pure Endowment part). It bundles both risk cover and savings.

questions.text: Which life insurance policy is best suited for a person whose primary goal is to leave a financial legacy or estate for their heirs?
options: Convertible Term Insurance
10-Year Endowment Plan
Decreasing Term Insurance
Whole Life Insurance
questions.explanation: Whole Life Insurance covers the policyholder for their entire life (usually up to 100 years). The payout is guaranteed upon death, making it the perfect tool to leave an estate or legacy for the next generation.

questions.text: Mortgage Redemption Insurance (MRI), generally used to protect home loans, is a practical application of which type of policy?
options: Increasing Term Insurance
Decreasing Term Insurance
Level Term Insurance
Whole Life Insurance
questions.explanation: In a Decreasing Term Insurance, the Sum Assured gradually reduces over time, perfectly matching the reducing outstanding principal amount of a home loan (mortgage).

questions.text: Under a standard 'Money-Back' policy, what happens to the death benefit if the insured dies after having already received two periodic survival benefits?
options: The policy is cancelled and only the remaining premiums are refunded.
The death benefit is reduced by subtracting the survival benefits already paid.
The full original Sum Assured is paid without deducting the already paid survival benefits.
Only the accumulated bonuses are paid.
questions.explanation: This is the most critical feature of a Money-Back policy. Despite paying periodic survival benefits, if the insured dies, the insurance company pays the full Sum Assured to the nominee without any deductions.

questions.text: What does a 'Participating' (Par) life insurance policy mean?
options: The policyholder gets a share in the profits of the insurance company in the form of bonuses.
The policyholder can participate in the management decisions of the company.
The premium changes every month based on the stock market.
Multiple family members can participate in a single policy.
questions.explanation: Participating or 'With-Profit' policies are those where the insurance company shares its valuation surplus (profits) with the policyholders by declaring annual bonuses.

questions.text: A policy that allows the insured to change a term insurance plan into an endowment plan after a few years without a fresh medical test is called:
options: Increasing Term Assurance
Money-Back Assurance
Return of Premium Plan
Convertible Term Assurance
questions.explanation: Convertible term policies allow the insured to 'convert' their pure risk cover into a savings-oriented plan (like Endowment) before a specific date, without undergoing any new medical underwriting.

questions.text: Which type of term insurance is designed specifically to combat the effects of inflation over a long period?
options: Level Term Assurance
Increasing Term Assurance
Decreasing Term Assurance
Renewable Term Assurance
questions.explanation: In an Increasing Term plan, the Sum Assured automatically increases by a fixed percentage every year. This helps the death benefit maintain its real purchasing power against inflation.

questions.text: Under a standard 'Whole Life' insurance policy, the death benefit is payable:
options: Only if death occurs before age 60.
Only if death is caused by an accident.
At any time death occurs, up to the age of 100 years.
Only after the payment of 20 annual premiums.
questions.explanation: Whole life policies have no fixed maturity date before the age of 100. The risk coverage continues throughout the lifetime of the insured, and the claim is paid whenever death occurs.

questions.text: What is the core feature of a 'Pure Endowment' contract?
options: It pays only if the insured dies in an accident.
It provides a lifelong pension.
It pays the nominee immediately if the insured dies.
It pays the benefit ONLY if the insured survives till the end of the specified term.
questions.explanation: A pure endowment only pays upon survival. If the insured dies before the term ends, nothing is paid. Because of this high risk to the customer, pure endowments are rarely sold alone and are always bundled with term insurance.

questions.text: Compared to an Endowment policy for the same Sum Assured and same entry age, the premium for a Term Insurance policy will be:
options: Substantially lower
Exactly the same
Slightly higher
Double the amount
questions.explanation: Term insurance contains no savings or investment element. Since the company only charges for the risk of death (mortality cost), the premium is drastically lower than an endowment policy.

questions.text: Additional optional benefits that can be attached to a base life insurance policy by paying a small extra premium are called:
options: Bonuses
Riders
Dividends
Endorsements
questions.explanation: Riders are add-on covers (like Accidental Death or Critical Illness) that customize and enhance the base policy's coverage for a nominal extra cost.

questions.text: If a policyholder becomes permanently disabled and cannot pay further premiums, which rider ensures that the policy continues to remain in force without any premium payments?
options: Accidental Death Benefit Rider
Critical Illness Rider
Waiver of Premium (WoP) Rider
Term Rider
questions.explanation: The Waiver of Premium rider guarantees that if the policyholder suffers total permanent disability, all future premiums are waived off (paid by the insurer), and the policy benefits remain fully active.

questions.text: Term insurance is most appropriate for a person who:
options: Needs maximum life cover at the lowest possible cost.
Wants a guaranteed lump sum for their child's marriage in 15 years.
Is looking for a regular monthly pension after retirement.
Wants to invest money for stock-market-linked returns.
questions.explanation: Because term insurance is cheap, it allows young earners with heavy liabilities and low budgets to buy massive coverage (like 1 Crore) to protect their family.

questions.text: In a standard Endowment policy, what does the policyholder receive upon surviving the maturity date?
options: Only the premiums paid are refunded.
A regular monthly pension for life.
Half of the Sum Assured.
The full Sum Assured plus accumulated bonuses (if it is a participating policy).
questions.explanation: Upon survival to maturity, an endowment policy pays the guaranteed Sum Assured along with any vested bonuses accumulated over the policy term.

questions.text: In a 'Limited Payment Whole Life Policy', how does the premium payment structure work?
options: Premiums are paid until the age of 100.
Premiums are paid for a specific shorter period, but the life cover continues for the entire lifetime.
Premiums are paid only in the year of death.
Only a single premium is paid at age 60.
questions.explanation: Limited Pay policies allow the insured to finish paying premiums during their earning years (e.g., pay for 15 years), while the life cover remains active until death.

questions.text: What is a common psychological limitation or objection people have towards buying pure term insurance?
options: The premium is too high to afford.
It requires them to undergo surgery.
They feel their money is 'wasted' if they survive, as there is no maturity return.
It does not pay the claim if the person dies.
questions.explanation: Because pure term plans do not refund any money upon survival, many customers incorrectly view it as a 'loss', not realizing they paid for peace of mind and pure risk transfer.

questions.text: In most traditional children's life insurance policies, the risk cover on the child's life usually commences:
options: On a specified deferred date or when the child reaches a certain age (e.g., 7 or 18 years).
Immediately on the day the child is born.
Only after the father's death.
Only when the child gets married.
questions.explanation: Because young children lack insurable value and have high early mortality, policies traditionally have a 'Deferment Period'. The actual life cover (risk) on the child starts at a later specified age.

questions.text: What is a 'Term Insurance with Return of Premium' (TROP) policy?
options: A policy that returns premiums every 5 years.
An endowment policy with double bonuses.
A term plan where the agent returns their commission.
A term plan that refunds all the base premiums paid if the insured survives till the end of the policy term.
questions.explanation: TROP is designed for customers who want term insurance but dislike the idea of zero maturity value. It offers pure life cover, but if you survive, the company returns the premiums you paid.

questions.text: Why do individuals often prefer a Money-Back policy over a standard Endowment policy?
options: Because Money-Back policies have zero premium.
Because it provides periodic liquidity to meet short-term financial goals before maturity.
Because the life cover decreases every year.
Because it is linked to the stock market.
questions.explanation: Endowment policies lock up funds until maturity. Money-back policies provide liquidity at regular intervals (e.g., every 5 years), helping families fund ongoing events like children's education.

questions.text: Policies that do not share in the valuation surplus (profits) of the insurer and have guaranteed benefits defined perfectly at the start are known as:
options: Non-Participating (Without-Profit) policies
Unit Linked Insurance Plans (ULIPs)
Convertible policies
Group Insurance policies
questions.explanation: Non-participating policies do not earn annual bonuses. The maturity and death benefits are strictly guaranteed upfront at the time of purchasing the policy.

questions.text: The Sum Assured in a Mortgage Redemption Insurance (MRI) policy reduces over time to strictly match the:
options: Age of the policyholder.
Value of the property in the market.
Outstanding principal amount of the home loan.
Inflation rate of the country.
questions.explanation: As you pay your home loan EMIs, the outstanding loan amount decreases. An MRI policy reduces its death benefit at the same rate, ensuring exactly enough money is available to clear the bank loan if the borrower dies.

questions.text: If a policy has a base Sum Assured of Rs. 10 Lakhs and an Accidental Death Benefit (ADB) rider of Rs. 5 Lakhs, how much is paid if the insured dies in a car accident?
options: Rs. 5 Lakhs
Rs. 10 Lakhs
Rs. 20 Lakhs
Rs. 15 Lakhs
questions.explanation: If the death is caused by an accident, the company pays the base Sum Assured (10 Lakhs) PLUS the rider benefit (5 Lakhs), making the total payout Rs. 15 Lakhs.

questions.text: A life insurance policy where the entire premium is paid as a one-time lump sum at the inception of the contract is called a:
options: Regular Premium Policy
Single Premium Policy
Limited Premium Policy
Monthly Premium Policy
questions.explanation: In a Single Premium policy, there are no recurring future payments. The policyholder pays a large lump sum upfront, which keeps the policy active for the entire term.

questions.text: The guaranteed amount that the insurance company promises to pay upon death or maturity is technically called the:
options: Premium
Surrender Value
Sum Assured
Paid-up Value
questions.explanation: The Sum Assured is the fundamental value of the contract. It is the minimum guaranteed amount that the insurer is legally bound to pay upon the occurrence of the insured event.
